narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your top-decile performance in Year 3 of school and your full-time commitment to studies. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 40.9% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold leading to your application's rejection—it's designed to prioritize students with the lowest predicted risk to ensure high bar passage rates. This decision was driven primarily by a few key factors from your profile compared to typical applicants. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, which is below the average of 3.21, stood out as the strongest push toward the failure prediction, outweighing other positives in the model's eyes. Similarly, your LSAT score of 32, lower than the typical 36.7, added some concern by nudging the prediction toward higher risk. On a brighter note, your exceptional Year 3 decile ranking at the top of your school (far above the average 5.53) was the most influential positive factor, strongly pulling the prediction toward success, with your Year 1 top decile (versus average 5.71) also helping significantly. Your middle family income bracket played a minor role in increasing the predicted risk, while your full-time status barely factored in. Overall, compared to typical students who pass the bar, your academic deciles shine brightly, but the model weighed your undergrad GPA and LSAT more heavily as red flags for bar performance. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman trailblazer in law—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score or strengthening your GPA through post-baccalaureate courses, then reapply with those updates to shift the model's view. We're rooting for you to turn this into a success story.
